h1Title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE): Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic systemic autoimmune disease in which immune complexes formed by anti-nuclear autoantibodies deposit in multiple organs, driving inflammation in the skin, joints, kidneys, brain, lungs, and heart. SLE affects approximately 161,000-322,000 people in the United States, with a global prevalence of 20-150 per 100,000. Women of childbearing age are disproportionately affected at a 9:1 female-to-male ratio; Black, Hispanic, and Asian women have 2-3-fold higher incidence than White women. The classic malar rash and joint pain are early clues, but lupus nephritis — occurring in 40-60% of patients — is the primary driver of long-term morbidity. Hydroxychloroquine is prescribed to virtually all patients; belimumab and voclosporin represent modern targeted additions to the treatment arsenal.

keyStats.prevalence: 161,000-322,000 prevalent cases in the US (Helmick et al., Arthritis Rheum 2008); global prevalence 20-150 per 100,000
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio 9:1 overall; Black women have 3-fold higher incidence than White women; onset predominantly in childbearing years (ages 15-45)
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at diagnosis 28-32 years in most population-based cohorts; can occur at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 5 million people living with SLE worldwide (Lupus Foundation of America 2022)

definition: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE; ICD-10: M32) is a chronic, relapsing-remitting systemic autoimmune disease characterized by the production of autoantibodies against nuclear antigens — particularly double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) and Smith antigen (Sm) — and the deposition of immune complexes that activate complement and drive multi-organ inflammation. The 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria use a weighted scoring system across 10 domains (antinuclear antibodies, fever, hematological, neuropsychiatric, mucocutaneous, serosal, musculoskeletal, renal, antiphospholipid, and complement) with a threshold of 10 points or more plus positive ANA at entry criterion. Disease activity is measured by validated scores including SLEDAI-2K and BILAG-2004. SLE requires hydroxychloroquine as background therapy in all patients without contraindication, per ACR 2019 and EULAR 2023 guidelines. Kidney involvement (lupus nephritis classes I-VI by ISN/RPS 2018 classification) occurs in 40-60% and is the primary driver of permanent organ damage when inadequately treated. Neuropsychiatric SLE (NPSLE) — including seizures, stroke, cognitive impairment, and psychosis — occurs in 25-50% and significantly worsens prognosis. SLE is managed primarily by rheumatologists with nephrology, neurology, dermatology, cardiology, and obstetrics input according to organ manifestations.

typesClassification.description: Mucocutaneous and musculoskeletal manifestations without major organ involvement. Includes malar rash, discoid lupus, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, arthritis, and fatigue. Managed with hydroxychloroquine and topical/low-dose systemic steroids.
Autoimmune cytopenia (hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, leucopenia), serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis), and systemic symptoms requiring corticosteroids and steroid-sparing DMARDs.

Proliferative nephritis (Class III or IV) presents with hematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, and falling GFR. Requires high-dose corticosteroids plus mycophenolate mofetil or low-dose cyclophosphamide induction, followed by maintenance therapy.

Seizures, stroke, mononeuritis multiplex, cognitive dysfunction, or psychosis attributable to SLE. Requires combined immunosuppressive and anti-thrombotic management depending on the specific neuropsychiatric manifestation.

SLE-like syndrome triggered by medications (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, TNF inhibitors). Features anti-histone antibody, pleuritis, and arthritis; rarely causes nephritis. Resolves on drug discontinuation.

primarySymptoms: Malar rash — a butterfly-shaped erythematous rash across the cheeks and nose bridge sparing the nasolabial folds — present in 40-50% of patients and often photosensitive.
Polyarthritis of small and large joints with morning stiffness; non-erosive in most patients but Jaccoud arthropathy (reducible deformity from capsular laxity) occurs in 5-10%.
Profound fatigue affecting 80-90% of patients, often disproportionate to disease activity scores, persisting even during remission.
Photosensitivity — exaggerated skin reactions to UV light causing rash flares, constitutional symptoms, or internal organ flares after sun exposure.
Oral ulcers typically on the hard palate, painless or mildly tender, present during active disease phases.
Alopecia — diffuse non-scarring hair loss during disease flares, and scarring alopecia from discoid lupus lesions on the scalp.
Serositis — pleuritis causing pleuritic chest pain, dry cough, and pleural effusion, or pericarditis with positional chest pain and pericardial rub.
Raynaud phenomenon with triphasic color change, present in approximately 25% of SLE patients.
Hematological complications: hemolytic anemia (positive Coombs test), leukopenia, lymphopenia, and thrombocytopenia.
Nephrotic syndrome presentation — foamy urine, peripheral edema, hypertension, and weight gain — in patients with proliferative or membranous lupus nephritis.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset butterfly facial rash after sun exposure in a young woman, especially with joint pain, fatigue, or mouth sores — prompt ANA testing is warranted.
Unexplained cytopenias (leucopenia below 4,000/µL or lymphopenia below 1,000/µL or hemolytic anemia) in a young woman with constitutional symptoms.
New proteinuria or hematuria on dipstick in a patient with known positive ANA — suggests early lupus nephritis requiring urgent urine protein-creatinine ratio and renal biopsy.
Recurrent pregnancy loss or venous thrombosis with positive lupus anticoagulant or anticardiolipin antibody — antiphospholipid syndrome must be excluded, which frequently co-occurs with SLE.
emergencySigns: New seizure, focal neurological deficit, or encephalopathy in a patient with known SLE — NPSLE, thrombotic stroke from antiphospholipid syndrome, or CNS vasculitis requires emergency MRI and lumbar puncture.
Rapidly rising serum creatinine with hematuria and proteinuria — active crescentic or proliferative lupus nephritis requires urgent renal biopsy and high-dose immunosuppression.
Acute dyspnea with hypoxia and bilateral infiltrates — diffuse alveolar hemorrhage or catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome is life-threatening and requires ICU-level care.

Acute chest pain with ST elevation or rising troponin — lupus myocarditis or Libman-Sacks endocarditis with embolism must be distinguished from atherosclerotic coronary disease, which is accelerated 5-10-fold in SLE.

causes.cause: Loss of tolerance to nuclear antigens
causes.description: Defective clearance of apoptotic cells and NETs (neutrophil extracellular traps) exposes nuclear material (dsDNA, histones, snRNP) to innate immune sensors. Plasmacytoid dendritic cells producing IFN-alpha and autoreactive B cells producing anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm antibodies are the central effectors. Immune complex deposition in glomeruli and vessels activates complement, recruits neutrophils, and drives organ damage.

A type I interferon signature (elevated expression of IFN-stimulated genes) is detectable in 60-80% of SLE patients and correlates with disease activity, nephritis risk, and anti-dsDNA levels. Innate nucleic acid sensors (TLR7, TLR9, cGAS-STING) stimulated by endogenous nucleic acid drive chronic IFN-alpha excess.

Homozygous deficiency of C1q, C2, or C4 is rare but confers the highest individual risk of SLE (90% lifetime risk for C1q deficiency). Common partial complement deficiency from low copy number of C4A gene contributes to genetic risk in the general population. Complement deficiency impairs apoptotic cell clearance.

More than 100 SLE susceptibility loci have been identified by GWAS. The strongest genetic associations are with HLA-DR2 (DRB1*1501) and HLA-DR3 (DRB1*0301), IRF5, STAT4, BLK, PTPN22, and the complement gene region. Monogenic SLE from defects in DNase I, TREX1, or SAMHD1 occurs in rare early-onset cases.

causes.cause: Sex hormones and the X chromosome
causes.description: Estrogen promotes B-cell survival, increases TLR7 expression, and reduces apoptotic cell clearance efficiency. The 9:1 female predominance and clustering around menarche, pregnancy, and OCP use are hallmarks. One X chromosome carries the TLR7 gene; men with Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) have SLE rates similar to women.

UV light directly damages DNA and promotes apoptotic cell nucleic acid exposure. Epstein-Barr virus encodes antigens that cross-react with Sm protein (molecular mimicry). Crystalline silica occupational exposure, smoking, and mercury exposure have been associated with elevated SLE risk in epidemiological studies.

riskFactors.description: Women 15-45 years account for 90% of SLE cases. Risk is highest during reproductive years and may increase with exogenous estrogen use. OCP use confers a modestly elevated risk (RR approximately 1.5).

Black women have 3-fold higher SLE incidence than White women with earlier onset, higher prevalence of lupus nephritis (50-60% vs 25% in White patients), and higher rates of end-stage renal disease. Hispanic and Asian women also have 2-fold elevated incidence.
First-degree relatives of SLE patients have an 8-10-fold elevated risk. Identical twin concordance is 24-65%; fraternal twin concordance 2-9%, confirming strong genetic component.
Current smoking increases SLE risk 1.5-2-fold and is associated with more severe mucocutaneous disease and reduced hydroxychloroquine efficacy. Smoking cessation is advised.
UV exposure triggers DNA damage and promotes apoptotic nucleic acid release; 70% of SLE patients have UV-triggered skin or systemic flares. Photoprotection reduces flare frequency.

Hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, minocycline, and TNF inhibitors can trigger lupus-like syndromes. Drug-induced lupus resolves on drug withdrawal but may unmask true SLE in susceptible individuals.
EBV DNA is more frequently detected in SLE patients than controls; EBV EBNA-1 cross-reacts with Sm antigen, providing a molecular mimicry mechanism. EBV reactivation correlates with SLE flares.

affectedDemographics: Women of childbearing age (15-45) constitute approximately 85-90% of SLE patients globally.
Black and Afro-Caribbean women have 3-fold higher incidence, earlier age of onset, and higher frequency of nephritis than White women.
Childhood-onset SLE (before age 16) represents 10-15% of all SLE and is associated with higher rates of nephritis (60-80%), neuropsychiatric disease, and more aggressive disease course.
Men with SLE are less common (10-15% of cases) but tend to have more serious organ involvement, particularly renal and CNS disease, at comparable activity scores.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is made using the 2019 EULAR/ACR classification criteria, which require positive ANA (titer ≥1:80) as an entry criterion followed by weighted scoring across 10 clinical and immunological domains. A score of 10 or more classifies the patient as SLE. In practice, the clinical workup begins with ANA — positive at high titer (≥1:80) in over 99% of SLE patients, though ANA positivity alone is non-specific (present in 5% of healthy adults). Specific autoantibodies confirm and refine the diagnosis: anti-dsDNA (positive in 70% of SLE, highly specific at 95%), anti-Sm (positive in 25-30%, essentially pathognomonic for SLE), antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2-GPI) in 30-40%. Complement levels C3 and C4 are typically low during active nephritis and increase during remission, making them useful activity markers. The urinalysis, protein-creatinine ratio (urine), and eGFR are fundamental screening tests: proteinuria above 0.5 g/24h or RBC casts in urine sediment indicate nephritis. Renal biopsy is performed to classify lupus nephritis (ISN/RPS Classes I-VI) and guide induction immunosuppression. Disease activity is measured by SLEDAI-2K or BILAG-2004 at every visit. Brain MRI with gadolinium and CSF analysis are used for neuropsychiatric manifestations. Periodic organ damage assessment using the SLICC Damage Index guides long-term prognosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screening test — must be positive (≥1:80) as entry criterion for 2019 EULAR/ACR criteria; >99% sensitivity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 2-3 days. ANA pattern (homogeneous suggests anti-dsDNA; speckled suggests anti-Sm or anti-RNP; peripheral suggests anti-dsDNA). A positive ANA is not specific for SLE alone.

diagnosticTests.purpose: High specificity for SLE; anti-dsDNA titer correlates with disease activity, especially nephritis; anti-Sm positivity is nearly pathognomonic
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw, part of the ENA panel and separate anti-dsDNA assay. Results in 5-7 days. Rising anti-dsDNA titer plus falling complement is a classic sign of approaching nephritis flare.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Low C3/C4 indicates active immune complex formation and complement consumption; normalizes during remission
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results same day. Used serially at every rheumatology visit alongside anti-dsDNA to guide treatment intensity.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with microscopy and protein-creatinine ratio
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for lupus nephritis — hematuria, casts, and proteinuria indicate nephritis requiring biopsy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine sample in a container. Microscopy results within hours; protein-creatinine ratio same day. Should be checked at every visit in all SLE patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies lupus nephritis class (I-VI, ISN/RPS 2018) to guide induction and maintenance immunosuppression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous needle biopsy under local anesthetic as a day procedure or short admission. Mild haematuria for 24-48 hours is common. Results take 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies antiphospholipid syndrome co-occurrence, major risk factor for thrombosis and pregnancy loss in SLE
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw; requires two positive results 12 weeks apart for APS classification. Lupus anticoagulant result takes 3-5 days. Positive results change anticoagulation and pregnancy management decisions.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of SLE is guided by disease activity, organ involvement, and prevention of long-term damage. Hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg/day) is prescribed to all SLE patients without contraindication — it reduces flare frequency by 50%, prevents organ damage accumulation, reduces thrombosis risk, improves survival, and is safe in pregnancy. The 2023 EULAR SLE management recommendations stratify treatment by organ domain. Low-activity non-organ-threatening disease is managed with hydroxychloroquine plus short-course NSAIDs or low-dose prednisone (≤7.5 mg/day). Methotrexate (10-20 mg/week) is added for arthritis and skin disease; azathioprine (2-3 mg/kg/day) for maintenance and skin/musculoskeletal involvement. Belimumab (200 mg subcutaneous weekly or 10 mg/kg IV monthly) is FDA and EMA approved as an add-on therapy for active serologically positive SLE; it reduces flare rates and organ damage accrual. Anifrolumab (300 mg IV monthly), an anti-interferon-alpha receptor antibody, received FDA approval in 2021 for moderate-to-severe SLE with active mucocutaneous and musculoskeletal disease. For lupus nephritis induction: the Euro-Lupus Protocol (low-dose cyclophosphamide 500 mg IV every 2 weeks for 6 pulses) or mycophenolate mofetil (3 g/day) with high-dose corticosteroids are equivalent in Class III/IV nephritis. Voclosporin (23.7 mg twice daily) added to mycophenolate + steroid produced superior early proteinuria remission (complete renal response 41% vs 23% at week 52) in the AURORA trial and received FDA approval for lupus nephritis in 2021. Maintenance immunosuppression with mycophenolate or azathioprine is continued for at least 3 years after nephritis induction.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: The cornerstone of SLE management for all patients. Inhibits TLR7/9 signaling, reduces type I interferon production, and prevents immune complex-driven inflammation. Reduces SLE flare frequency by ~50%, lowers cardiovascular event rates, and reduces infection risk. Annual ophthalmology review for maculopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 50% reduction in flare rates; 38% reduction in damage accrual over 5 years; reduced mortality in Canadian Lupus Cohort study

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil (2-3 g/day for induction; 1-2 g/day for maintenance)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction and maintenance therapy for lupus nephritis Class III/IV and V. Inhibits purine synthesis, blocking proliferating lymphocytes. Better tolerated than cyclophosphamide in most populations; equivalent efficacy in multinational trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete renal remission in 35-45% at 6 months; non-inferior to cyclophosphamide in ALMS and ACCESS trials

medicalTreatments.name: Belimumab (200 mg subcutaneous weekly or 10 mg/kg IV monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-BLyS/BAFF monoclonal antibody approved for active seropositive SLE and lupus nephritis. Reduces B-cell survival, lowering autoantibody production. Approved as add-on to standard therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe flares by ~51% versus placebo (BLISS-52, BLISS-76 trials); reduces nephritis flares and renal damage endpoints in the BLISS-LN trial

medicalTreatments.name: Voclosporin (23.7 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitor added to MMF + low-dose steroid for lupus nephritis. Reduces proteinuria faster and increases complete renal remission rates. FDA-approved 2021. Monitor blood pressure and creatinine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete renal response 41% vs 23% at 52 weeks (AURORA-1 trial); superior proteinuria reduction within 2 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Anifrolumab (300 mg IV every 4 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-interferon-alpha receptor monoclonal antibody targeting the type I IFN pathway. FDA-approved 2021 for moderate-to-severe non-nephritis SLE. Most effective in patients with high IFN gene signature scores.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TULIP-2 trial: 47.8% vs 31.5% BICLA response at 52 weeks; significant skin, joint, and steroid-sparing benefits

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (induction 0.5-1 mg/kg/day; maintenance lowest effective dose ≤7.5 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid disease control during flares; the 2023 EULAR guidelines recommend targeting ≤5 mg/day long-term or complete discontinuation to minimize damage from chronic corticosteroids. IV methylprednisolone pulses (500-1,000 mg for 3 days) used for severe organ-threatening disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid flare control in 60-80%; long-term damage from chronic steroids underscores the imperative to minimize dose

alternativeTreatments.name: Rituximab (off-label B-cell depletion)
alternativeTreatments.description: Anti-CD20 depletes B cells; used in refractory nephritis, severe refractory cytopenias, and NPSLE not responding to standard therapy. Not FDA-approved for SLE due to trial design issues but widely used in practice per EULAR guidance. Two doses of 500-1,000 mg IV 2 weeks apart.

whySeeSpecialist: A rheumatologist should assess all patients with suspected SLE to confirm the diagnosis, establish organ involvement, initiate hydroxychloroquine, and decide on the need for immunosuppressive therapy. Nephrologist co-management is essential when lupus nephritis is suspected; neurologist, cardiologist, or dermatologist input is required based on organ manifestations. Specialist-led monitoring reduces flares and damage accumulation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a rheumatologist experienced in systemic lupus with access to full autoantibody panels, complement measurement, and urine sediment microscopy. For lupus nephritis, a rheumatologist-nephrologist team at an academic center is ideal. For pregnancy planning, choose a center with a lupus-in-pregnancy clinic linking rheumatology and maternal-fetal medicine.
hospitalCriteria: Rheumatology service with full ANA and autoantibody panel access
Nephrology service with renal biopsy capability and lupus nephritis experience
Neurology service for NPSLE assessment with MRI and CSF analysis
Lupus pregnancy clinic with maternal-fetal medicine and rheumatology co-management
Dermatology for cutaneous lupus management and biopsies
keyFacilities: Renal biopsy programs with immunofluorescence and electron microscopy
Centers offering belimumab or anifrolumab infusions
preventionStrategies: Consistent daily photoprotection (SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen plus protective clothing) to minimize UV-triggered flares — established evidence in multiple cohort studies.
Smoking cessation: current smoking is associated with 1.5-2-fold elevated SLE incidence and more severe disease activity; quitting reduces these risks.
Maintain up-to-date vaccinations (annual inactivated influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19, shingles) to prevent infection-triggered flares and infection-related mortality in immunosuppressed patients.
Avoid medications known to trigger drug-induced lupus (hydralazine, procainamide, minocycline) when alternatives are available.

For women with SLE considering oral contraceptives: low-estrogen or progestin-only preparations are preferred; COCs with anti-phospholipid antibody positivity are contraindicated due to thrombosis risk.
lifestyleModifications: Never stop hydroxychloroquine without rheumatologist advice; discontinuation is associated with a 3-fold increase in flare rate.
Maintain a regular sleep schedule and manage fatigue with graded activity pacing; fatigue in SLE does not always reflect active inflammation and may be worsened by physical deconditioning.
Protect against UV light year-round including in winter and through glass; apply sunscreen to face, hands, and exposed areas every morning.
Maintain blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg — hypertension accelerates lupus nephritis progression and cardiovascular risk, which is 5-10-fold elevated in SLE.
Bone protection: calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-2,000 IU/day) in all patients on corticosteroids; bisphosphonate therapy if T-score below -1.5 on corticosteroids.
Cardioprotective lifestyle: low-saturated-fat diet, regular aerobic exercise, statin therapy in patients with elevated LDL or cardiovascular risk score.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean pattern) associated with reduced SLE disease activity in cross-sectional studies
Vitamin D-sufficient diet or supplementation (800-2,000 IU/day); vitamin D deficiency is nearly universal in SLE and associated with higher activity scores
Omega-3 fatty acids from oily fish or fish oil supplementation (1-3 g/day EPA+DHA) — small controlled trials show modest reduction in flare frequency
Calcium from dairy or fortified foods (1,000-1,200 mg/day) in patients on long-term corticosteroids
avoid: Alfalfa sprouts and supplements containing L-canavanine, which can trigger lupus flares through immune stimulation
High-sodium foods (processed meats, canned foods, fast food) — sodium worsens hypertension and promotes Th17-driven lupus nephritis in animal models
Excessive alcohol, which interacts with methotrexate and azathioprine, worsens GERD from NSAID use, and impairs medication adherence
Grapefruit when taking voclosporin or other calcineurin inhibitors (CYP3A4 interaction alters drug levels)

exerciseGuidelines: During active flares, rest and minimize physical stress. During remission, 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise is recommended — walking, swimming, and cycling reduce fatigue, depression, cardiovascular risk, and bone loss in SLE cohort studies. Resistance training twice weekly preserves muscle mass impaired by corticosteroids. Water aerobics is ideal for patients with joint disease. Avoid prolonged sun exposure during outdoor exercise.
dailyManagement: Take hydroxychloroquine daily at the same time, consistently; it acts prophylactically and is not effective on demand.
Apply SPF 50+ sunscreen every morning as a non-negotiable routine, including during winter; reapply after 2 hours of sun exposure.
Check urine with dipstick or protein strip periodically between visits if on nephritis maintenance therapy; increased protein or blood warrants prompt rheumatology contact.
Monitor blood pressure at home weekly if hypertensive or on steroids; target below 130/80 mmHg to protect kidneys and cardiovascular health.
Keep a symptom diary recording joint pain, rashes, fatigue, and any new symptoms to share with the rheumatologist at quarterly or 6-monthly visits.
prognosis: The prognosis of SLE has improved dramatically over the past 50 years. The 10-year survival in modern specialized-center cohorts exceeds 90-95%. The main causes of death are infections (40%), cardiovascular disease (25%), and active SLE (25%). The risk of cardiovascular events in SLE is 5-10-fold that of age-matched controls, driven by accelerated atherosclerosis from chronic inflammation, corticosteroid-induced metabolic effects, and antiphospholipid antibodies. Lupus nephritis is the primary determinant of long-term renal outcomes: 10-25% of patients with Class III/IV nephritis reach end-stage renal disease over 20 years despite treatment. Patients who achieve complete renal remission by 12 months have superior long-term outcomes. SLICC Damage Index accrual (reflecting cumulative end-organ damage) is the strongest predictor of long-term disability and mortality. Pregnancy-associated flares occur in 40-65% of pregnancies; hydroxychloroquine continuation through pregnancy reduces this rate. Women who achieve disease remission for at least 6 months before conception have substantially better obstetric outcomes.
recoveryTimeline: Flares: constitutional and musculoskeletal flares respond within 1-4 weeks of adjusted therapy. Nephritis induction: proteinuria begins falling within 4-8 weeks; complete renal response by 12 months in 40-60%. Maintenance phase is continued for at least 3 years. Stable SLE monitoring: rheumatology every 3-6 months; nephrology every 3-6 months when nephritis is active.
complications: Lupus nephritis progressing to end-stage renal disease in 10-25% of Class III/IV nephritis patients over 20 years — track proteinuria and GFR at every visit.

Accelerated atherosclerosis: cardiovascular event rates 5-10-fold higher than the general population; cardiovascular disease accounts for 25% of SLE-related deaths — manage Framingham risk factors aggressively.
Neuropsychiatric SLE (seizures, stroke, cognitive decline, psychosis) in 25-50% of patients; chronic cognitive impairment may persist despite disease control.
Antiphospholipid syndrome co-occurring in 30-40% — venous thromboembolism, arterial stroke, and recurrent pregnancy loss requiring long-term anticoagulation.
Corticosteroid-related damage: osteoporosis, avascular necrosis (particularly femoral head in 10-15% of patients on long-term steroids), cataracts, diabetes, and hypertension.
Infection: 40% of SLE-related mortality; annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccination plus prompt antibiotic treatment of suspected infection are mandatory.

supportResources.name: Lupus Foundation of America
supportResources.description: US national patient organization with helpline, local chapters, and education on managing SLE, flares, and pregnancy.

supportResources.name: Lupus UK
supportResources.description: UK patient organization providing support, publications, and hospital liaison nurse network.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — SLE Patient Resources
supportResources.description: ACR patient education on SLE, treatment guidelines, and finding a rheumatologist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RA causes symmetric erosive polyarthritis predominantly at MCP and PIP joints with RF and anti-CCP positivity in 70-80%. SLE arthritis is non-erosive in most cases and is accompanied by systemic features, positive anti-dsDNA, cytopenias, and nephritis. ANA positivity in RA is typically low-titer without the specific anti-Sm or anti-dsDNA seen in SLE.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MCTD requires high-titer anti-U1 RNP antibody with overlap features of SLE, SSc, and polymyositis. It typically causes milder nephritis and lacks the strong anti-dsDNA or anti-Sm antibody dominance of SLE. About 25-35% of MCTD patients eventually fulfill full SLE criteria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic Sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SSc presents with progressive skin fibrosis, Raynaud phenomenon, ILD, and anti-Scl-70 or anticentromere antibodies. SLE skin involvement is inflammatory (malar rash, photosensitivity) rather than fibrotic. Anti-dsDNA and lupus nephritis are absent in pure SSc.

confusedWithConditions.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: APS causes arterial and venous thrombosis and pregnancy morbidity from antiphospholipid antibodies; it can occur alone or co-exist with SLE in 30-40% of SLE patients. SLE encompasses a much broader spectrum of organ involvement beyond thrombosis.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus Nephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Major organ complication occurring in 40-60% of SLE patients; the primary determinant of long-term renal and overall prognosis.

relatedConditions.name: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Overlap syndrome sharing SLE features defined by anti-U1 RNP antibody; 25-35% of MCTD patients evolve to SLE.

relatedConditions.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-occurs in 30-40% of SLE; drives thrombosis and pregnancy loss; requires separate anticoagulation management.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently co-occurs with SLE (secondary Sjogren); sicca symptoms and anti-SSA/SSB positivity are shared features.

faqs.question: What is systemic lupus erythematosus?
faqs.answer: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks the body's own tissues, causing inflammation in the skin, joints, kidneys, brain, and other organs. It is defined by antinuclear antibodies including anti-dsDNA. About 90% of patients are women of childbearing age.

faqs.question: What is the butterfly rash of lupus?
faqs.answer: The malar rash is a butterfly-shaped erythema across both cheeks and the bridge of the nose that spares the nasolabial folds. It is present in 40-50% of SLE patients and is typically photosensitive, appearing or worsening after sun exposure. It does not scar but can persist for days or weeks during a flare.

faqs.question: What causes lupus?
faqs.answer: SLE results from a combination of genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR2, DR3, and over 100 genetic risk loci), hormonal factors (estrogen promotes B-cell survival), and environmental triggers including UV light, Epstein-Barr virus infection, and smoking. These combine to break immune tolerance to nuclear antigens, driving autoantibody production.

faqs.question: How is lupus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses the 2019 EULAR/ACR criteria: positive ANA (≥1:80) is required, then a weighted score across 10 domains reaches 10 points or more. Key tests include anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, complement C3/C4, CBC for cytopenias, and urine protein-creatinine ratio to screen for nephritis.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for lupus?
faqs.answer: Hydroxychloroquine (200-400 mg/day) is the foundation of SLE treatment for all patients — it reduces flare frequency by 50% and improves survival. Corticosteroids control active flares. Mycophenolate mofetil treats lupus nephritis. Belimumab and anifrolumab are approved add-on biologics for active seropositive disease.

faqs.question: Does lupus affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lupus nephritis occurs in 40-60% of SLE patients, most commonly in Black and Hispanic women. It presents with protein and blood in the urine and can progress to kidney failure. Renal biopsy classifies the type and guides treatment. Mycophenolate mofetil and voclosporin are first-line for active nephritis.

faqs.question: Is lupus hereditary?
faqs.answer: SLE has a strong genetic component with over 100 identified susceptibility loci. First-degree relatives of SLE patients have an 8-10-fold elevated risk. However, twin concordance is only 24-65%, so environmental triggers are essential co-factors. Most SLE cases are sporadic rather than familial.

faqs.question: Can lupus be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for SLE. Treatment suppresses immune activity, prevents organ damage, and reduces flare frequency. Many patients achieve sustained remission on hydroxychloroquine with or without additional DMARDs. The goal is the lowest possible disease activity with minimal corticosteroid exposure.

faqs.question: What triggers a lupus flare?
faqs.answer: Common flare triggers include UV light exposure, infections, emotional stress, pregnancy, certain medications (OCP, minocycline), and stopping hydroxychloroquine. Consistent photoprotection, hydroxychloroquine adherence, and prompt antibiotic treatment of infections are the main preventable factors.

faqs.question: Is lupus more common in women?
faqs.answer: Yes. The female-to-male ratio is approximately 9:1, the highest sex predilection of any major systemic autoimmune disease. The disparity is most pronounced in the 15-45 age group. Men with SLE tend to have more severe renal and CNS involvement at onset.

faqs.question: Can you get pregnant with lupus?
faqs.answer: Yes, but pregnancy in SLE requires planning. Disease should be in remission for at least 6 months before conception. Flares occur in 40-65% of SLE pregnancies. Hydroxychloroquine is safe and should be continued. Methotrexate and mycophenolate must be stopped before conception. Lupus pregnancies are co-managed by rheumatology and maternal-fetal medicine.

faqs.question: What does anti-dsDNA mean in lupus?
faqs.answer: Anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) antibody is detected in 70% of SLE patients and is highly specific for the diagnosis. Rising anti-dsDNA titers alongside falling complement levels often predict a lupus flare, especially nephritis. Serial anti-dsDNA measurement guides treatment intensification decisions.

faqs.question: What is lupus nephritis?
faqs.answer: Lupus nephritis is kidney inflammation caused by SLE immune-complex deposition in the glomeruli. It affects 40-60% of SLE patients and presents with proteinuria, hematuria, and rising creatinine. Untreated, it can lead to end-stage renal disease. Renal biopsy classifies the severity; mycophenolate mofetil is the main treatment.

faqs.question: Does lupus affect life expectancy?
faqs.answer: 10-year survival exceeds 90% in modern specialized-center cohorts. Main causes of death are infections (40%), cardiovascular disease (25%), and active SLE (25%). Cardiovascular risk is 5-10-fold elevated versus the general population. Early treatment, tight disease control, and aggressive cardiovascular risk factor management are the key survival determinants.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SLE and drug-induced lupus?
faqs.answer: Drug-induced lupus is triggered by medications (hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, TNF inhibitors) and features anti-histone antibody, arthritis, and serositis. It rarely causes nephritis or anti-dsDNA positivity. It resolves within weeks to months of stopping the causative drug. True SLE persists indefinitely.

metaTitle: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE): autoimmune disease affecting skin, kidneys, joints and brain. Symptoms, anti-dsDNA diagnosis, and treatment reviewed.
